text: To experimentally test whether loss of CEBPA mRNA alters HK3 and KLF5 expression, we knocked down CEBPA in the two APL cell line models NB4 and HT93. This was accomplished using lentiviral vectors expressing two independent short hairpin (sh)RNAs targeting the CEBPA gene. Knockdown efficiency was evaluated by qPCR and Western blotting ( Figures 2a, e and f ). After 4 days of treatment with 1 μM ATRA to promote neutrophil differentiation, we observed significantly reduced HK3 and KLF5 mRNA levels in the NB4 and HT93 CEBPA knockdown cell lines as compared to the control cells ( Figures 2b and c ). We evaluated G-CSFR expression in CEBPA knockdown cells ( Figure 2d ) to validate inhibition of ATRA-induced neutrophil differentiation, while induction of CEBPE , a direct CEBPA regulated gene 17 , was measured as an additional control ( Supplementary Figures S1a and b ). Our results clearly demonstrate that the upregulation of HK3 and KLF5 during neutrophil differentiation of APL cells is CEBPA -dependent.
summary: In APL cell lines NB4 and HT93 treated with 1 μM ATRA for 4 days to induce neutrophil differentiation, CEBPA was examined for regulation of HK3 and KLF5. Lentiviral shRNA-mediated knockdown of CEBPA reduced HK3 and KLF5 mRNA levels relative to controls. Thus, the upregulation of HK3 and KLF5 during neutrophil differentiation of these APL cells is CEBPA-dependent.

text: The basic leucine zipper transcription factor CCAAT/enhancer binding protein alpha ( CEBPA ) codes for a critical regulator during neutrophil differentiation. Aberrant expression or function of this protein contributes to the development of acute myeloid leukemia (AML). In this study, we identified two novel unrelated CEBPA target genes, the glycolytic enzyme hexokinase 3 ( HK3 ) and the krüppel-like factor 5 ( KLF5 ) transcription factor, by comparing gene profiles in two cohorts of CEBPA wild-type and mutant AML patients. In addition, we found CEBPA-dependent activation of HK3 and KLF5 transcription during all- trans retinoic acid (ATRA) mediated neutrophil differentiation of acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) cells. Moreover, we observed direct regulation of HK3 by CEBPA, whereas our data suggest an indirect regulation of KLF5 by this transcription factor. Altogether, our data provide an explanation for low HK3 and KLF5 expression in particular AML subtype and establish these genes as novel CEBPA targets during neutrophil differentiation.
summary: CEBPA is a critical regulator of neutrophil differentiation and its aberrant expression or function contributes to acute myeloid leukemia. By comparing gene profiles in cohorts of CEBPA wild-type and mutant AML patients the study identified HK3 and KLF5 as novel CEBPA targets and found CEBPA-dependent activation of their transcription during ATRA-mediated neutrophil differentiation of APL cells. The data indicate direct regulation of HK3 by CEBPA and suggest indirect regulation of KLF5 by CEBPA. These findings explain low HK3 and KLF5 expression in a particular AML subtype.

text: The CCAAT/enhancer binding protein alpha ( CEBPA ) is a master regulator of myeloid differentiation that drives hematopoiesis towards the granulocytic lineage 1 . The relevance of CEBPA in the maintenance of myeloid lineage is underlined by the fact that Cebpa-null mice lack the production of neutrophils and eosinophils 2 . Not surprisingly, CEBPA functions are frequently disrupted in particular subtypes of acute myeloid leukemias (AML), thereby contributing to the differentiation arrest seen in AML blast cells. Several groups reported aberrant CEBPA function due to intragenetic mutations in 5–10% of AML patients (10–19% in normal karyotype-AML) (Reviewed in 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 ). Mutations in the CEBPA gene predominantly cause either a frame-shift at the N-terminus resulting in the truncated CEBPA isoform p30 with dominant negative function, or disrupt the basic zipper region at the C-terminus causing weakened DNA-binding (Reviewed in 7 , 8 ). Furthermore, most AML patients harbor CEBPA mutations on both alleles and these bi-allelic CEBPA mutated patients have a favorable clinical outcome as compared to CEBPA -wild-type or patients with a mono-allelic mutation (Reviewed in 5 , 6 ). Lastly, transcriptional repression mediated by leukemic fusion proteins or epigenetic alterations such as aberrant promoter hypermethylation further leads to low CEBPA expression in some AML patients 9 .
summary: CEBPA is a master regulator of myeloid differentiation that drives hematopoiesis toward the granulocytic lineage. In AML, intragenic CEBPA mutations create an N‑terminal frameshift that produces a dominant-negative p30 isoform. Other mutations disrupt the C‑terminal basic zipper and weaken DNA binding. Transcriptional repression by leukemic fusion proteins or promoter hypermethylation also leads to low CEBPA expression in some AML patients. These disruptions contribute to differentiation arrest in AML blast cells and Cebpa-null mice lack neutrophil and eosinophil production.
